name: French A1 Grammar
description: CEFR A1 French grammar, scoped from the Référentiel A1 (Beacco et al., Didier). Fill in the blank.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} l'anglais.
(I'm studying English.)
facts.explanation: étudie — je form of étudier (to study). Regular -er verb: étudi- + -e. Je elides to j' before the vowel é.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il {___} anglais.
(He is speaking English.)
facts.explanation: parle — il/elle form of parler (to speak). Regular -er verb: parl- + -e. Identical in form to the je person.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}-tu italien ?
(Do you speak Italian?)
facts.explanation: parles — tu form of parler. Regular -er: parl- + -es. In inverted questions the verb comes first: Parles-tu?

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} anglais.
(Let's speak English.)
facts.explanation: parlons — nous form of parler. Regular -er: parl- + -ons. The nous form also serves as a first-person plural imperative (Parlons! = Let's speak!).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}-vous anglais ?
(Do you speak English?)
facts.explanation: parlez — vous form of parler. Regular -er: parl- + -ez. One of the first phrases a francophone traveller learns.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu {___} dur.
(You work hard.)
facts.explanation: travailles — tu form of travailler (to work). Regular -er verb: travaill- + -es.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il {___} bien.
(He works well.)
facts.explanation: travaille — il form of travailler. Regular -er verb: travaill- + -e. The double-l stem is entirely regular.

facts.quizQuestion:
Vous {___} dur.
(You work hard.)
facts.explanation: travaillez — vous form of travailler. Regular -er: travaill- + -ez. Use vous for formal address or when speaking to multiple people.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} les chiens.
(I like dogs.)
facts.explanation: aime — je form of aimer (to like/love). Regular -er verb: aim- + -e. Je elides to j' before the vowel a.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} apprendre l'anglais.
(I like to study English.)
facts.explanation: aime — je form of aimer. When followed by an infinitive (apprendre), aimer + infinitif means 'to like to do something' — a core A1 construction.

facts.quizQuestion:
Qu'{___}-tu ?
(What do you like?)
facts.explanation: aimes — tu form of aimer. Regular -er: aim- + -es. Inverted question form: aimes-tu = do you like.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} à Kakogawa.
(I live in Kakogawa.)
facts.explanation: habite — je form of habiter (to live/reside). Regular -er verb: habit- + -e. Je elides to j' before vowels including h aspiré vs muet — habiter has a mute h.

facts.quizQuestion:
Où {___}-tu ?
(Where do you live?)
facts.explanation: habites — tu form of habiter. Regular -er: habit- + -es. Essential question for introductions at A1 level.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} français tous les jours.
(I speak French every day.)
facts.explanation: parle — je form of parler (to speak). Regular -er verb: parl- + -e. Note je and il/elle share the same spelling but pronunciation is identical.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} très vite.
(They speak very fast.)
facts.explanation: parlent — ils/elles form of parler. Regular -er: parl- + -ent. The -ent ending is always silent in spoken French.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} dans un bureau.
(I work in an office.)
facts.explanation: travaille — je form of travailler (to work). Regular -er: travaill- + -e. Same spelling as il/elle form.

facts.quizQuestion:
Mon grand-père {___} marcher.
(My grandfather likes to walk.)
facts.explanation: aime — il form of aimer. Identical to the je form: aim- + -e. Context (the subject noun phrase) makes the person clear.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} le français chez moi.
(I study French at home.)
facts.explanation: étudie — je form of étudier (to study). Regular -er: étudi- + -e. The -ier ending is not irregular; standard -er conjugation applies.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il {___} à Paris.
(He lives in Paris.)
facts.explanation: habite — il form of habiter. Regular -er: habit- + -e. The je and il/elle forms are spelled and pronounced identically.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} la musique française.
(We love French music.)
facts.explanation: aimons — nous form of aimer. Regular -er: aim- + -ons. All regular -er verbs use -ons for the nous form.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} à Lyon.
(We live in Lyon.)
facts.explanation: habitons — nous form of habiter. Regular -er: habit- + -ons. Used to say where a group or family lives.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} ensemble tous les jours.
(They work together every day.)
facts.explanation: travaillent — ils/elles form of travailler. Regular -er: travaill- + -ent. The -ent suffix is silent; the stem pronounces as 'travay'.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} le sport.
(They like sport.)
facts.explanation: aiment — ils/elles form of aimer. Regular -er: aim- + -ent. The -ent is silent; sounds like 'aime' in speech.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} le français en classe.
(We study French in class.)
facts.explanation: étudions — nous form of étudier. Regular -er: étudi- + -ons. The -ier ending treats identically to any other -er verb.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} heureux.
(I am happy.)
facts.explanation: suis — je form of être (to be). Full paradigm: suis/es/est/sommes/êtes/sont.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} étudiant.
(I am a student.)
facts.explanation: suis — je form of être. Use être to describe identity and states: je suis étudiant.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} curieux.
(I am curious.)
facts.explanation: suis — je form of être. Être + adjective expresses both permanent traits and temporary states.

facts.quizQuestion:
Où {___}-tu ?
(Where are you?)
facts.explanation: es — tu form of être. Note the inversion: tu es → es-tu in questions.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu {___} prêt ?
(Are you ready?)
facts.explanation: es — tu form of être. Do not confuse with est (il/elle form) — different subjects, different forms.

facts.quizQuestion:
Elle {___} heureuse.
(She is happy.)
facts.explanation: est — il/elle form of être. Same form for both il and elle: il est / elle est.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il {___} enseignant.
(He is a teacher.)
facts.explanation: est — il/elle form of être. Professions after être take no article: il est enseignant.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} lundi.
(It's Monday.)
facts.explanation: sommes — nous form of être. Nous sommes + day of week is the standard way to state the day in French.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} amis.
(We are friends.)
facts.explanation: sommes — nous form of être. Unique form — do not confuse with regular -ons endings on other verbs.

facts.quizQuestion:
Vous {___} prêts ?
(Are you ready?)
facts.explanation: êtes — vous form of être. Note the circumflex: êtes (not *êtez). Used for plural or formal vous.

facts.quizQuestion:
Vous {___} français ?
(Are you French?)
facts.explanation: êtes — vous form of être. The circumflex accent (ê) sets êtes apart from regular -ez verb endings.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} fatigués.
(They are tired.)
facts.explanation: sont — ils/elles form of être. Same form for both ils and elles: ils sont / elles sont.

facts.quizQuestion:
Elles {___} françaises.
(They are French.)
facts.explanation: sont — ils/elles form of être. Masculine ils and feminine elles share the same verb form sont.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} soif.
(I'm thirsty.)
facts.explanation: ai — je form of avoir (to have). Avoir + soif/faim/froid/chaud expresses physical states in French.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} un rêve.
(I have a dream.)
facts.explanation: ai — je form of avoir. Full paradigm: ai/as/a/avons/avez/ont.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} un chien.
(I have a dog.)
facts.explanation: ai — je form of avoir. Note the mandatory elision: je ai contracts to j'ai before a vowel.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} deux filles.
(I have two daughters.)
facts.explanation: ai — je form of avoir. Avoir expresses possession: j'ai + noun.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu {___} raison.
(You're right.)
facts.explanation: as — tu form of avoir. Avoir raison/tort means to be right/wrong — French uses avoir, not être.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu {___} faim ?
(Are you hungry?)
facts.explanation: as — tu form of avoir. Avoir faim (to be hungry) is an idiomatic expression — avoid literal translation.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il {___} un frère.
(He has a brother.)
facts.explanation: a — il/elle form of avoir. Do not confuse a (verb, no accent) with à (preposition, with accent).

facts.quizQuestion:
Elle {___} vingt ans.
(She is twenty years old.)
facts.explanation: a — il/elle form of avoir. Age uses avoir in French: elle a vingt ans (not elle est vingt ans).

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} un chat.
(We have a cat.)
facts.explanation: avons — nous form of avoir. Regular -ons ending on the stem av-: av + ons = avons.

facts.quizQuestion:
Vous {___} de la chance.
(You are lucky.)
facts.explanation: avez — vous form of avoir. Avoir de la chance (to be lucky) is another case where avoir replaces être.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} deux enfants.
(They have two children.)
facts.explanation: ont — ils/elles form of avoir. Note the liaison: ils ont /ilz-ɔ̃/. Same form for both ils and elles.

facts.quizQuestion:
Elles {___} raison.
(They are right.)
facts.explanation: ont — ils/elles form of avoir. Masculine ils and feminine elles share the same form: ont.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} au marché.
(We go to the market.)
facts.explanation: allons — nous form of aller. Irregular: vais/vas/va/allons/allez/vont.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} à l'école.
(They go to school.)
facts.explanation: vont — ils form of aller. Memorize: vais/vas/va/allons/allez/vont.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quel âge {___}-tu ?
(What is your age?)
facts.explanation: To ask someone's age in French: Quel âge as-tu ? (literally 'What age do you have?') — French uses avoir, not être.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je n'{___} pas de voiture.
(I don't have a car.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: avoir (je) in negation — de replaces un/une after ne…pas
facts.explanation: After ne…pas, the indefinite article un/une/des becomes de: je n'ai pas de voiture (not une voiture).

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} besoin d'aide.
(I need help.)
facts.explanation: Avoir besoin de + infinitive/noun means 'to need': j'ai besoin d'aide, j'ai besoin de dormir.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} vingt ans.
(I am twenty years old.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: avoir + age in years — j'ai … ans
facts.explanation: Age is expressed with avoir + number + ans: j'ai vingt ans. Never use être for age in French.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} faim.
(I'm hungry.)
facts.explanation: Like avoir soif (thirsty) and avoir peur (scared), avoir faim (hungry) uses avoir for a physical or emotional state.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} Jack.
(My name is Jack.)
facts.explanation: S'appeler is a reflexive verb: je m'appelle. The reflexive pronoun me elides to m' before a vowel.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} Edgar Degas.
(My name is Edgar Degas.)
facts.explanation: Je m'appelle is the standard way to introduce yourself in French; it is more natural than Mon nom est…

facts.quizQuestion:
Je dois {___} tôt.
(I have to get up early.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I have to get up early.
facts.explanation: After a modal like devoir, the reflexive verb stays in the infinitive: devoir + se réveiller. The reflexive pronoun (me) agrees with the subject.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} une belle journée.
(It's a beautiful day.)
facts.explanation: C'est (ce + est) is used before an article + noun to identify or describe: c'est une belle journée.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} moi.
(It's me.)
facts.explanation: C'est + tonic pronoun is used to identify people: c'est moi, c'est lui, c'est elle. Never use subject pronouns here.

facts.quizQuestion:
Bien. Et {___} ?
(Fine. And you?)
facts.grammarPointLabel: tonic pronoun — et toi ? (isolated / after conjunction)
facts.explanation: Tonic pronouns (moi, toi, lui…) are used after prepositions or alone, e.g., Et toi ? — you cannot say Et tu ?

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} aussi, j'aime l'anglais.
(I like English, too.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: moi aussi — tonic pronoun for emphasis
facts.explanation: Moi aussi (me too) uses the tonic pronoun moi for emphasis at the start of a sentence. Aussi follows the tonic pronoun.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} lave le matin.
(I wash myself in the morning.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I wash myself in the morning.
facts.explanation: me — first person reflexive pronoun before consonant. Je + me = Je me lave.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu {___} réveilles à sept heures.
(You wake up at seven.)
facts.explanation: te — second person reflexive pronoun. Tu + te = Tu te réveilles.

facts.quizQuestion:
Elle {___} couche tôt.
(She goes to bed early.)
facts.explanation: se — third person reflexive pronoun. Il/elle + se.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} mes devoirs.
(I'm finishing my homework.)
facts.explanation: finis — je form of finir (to finish). Finir-type -ir verbs add -ss- in plural: fin- + -is → finis. Distinguished from partir-type, which drops the final consonant instead.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu {___} ton repas ?
(Are you finishing your meal?)
facts.explanation: finis — tu form of finir. Finir-type -ir verbs: fin- + -is (tu and je share the same ending). The -iss- infix appears only in plural forms.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} le travail ensemble.
(We are finishing the work together.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We are finishing the work together.
facts.explanation: finissons — nous form of finir. The -iss- infix appears in all plural forms of finir-type verbs: fin- + -iss- + -ons.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} leurs devoirs avant le dîner.
(They finish their homework before dinner.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: They finish their homework before dinner.
facts.explanation: finissent — ils/elles form of finir. The -iss- infix marks finir-type verbs in plural: fin- + -iss- + -ent.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} une glace au chocolat.
(I'm choosing a chocolate ice cream.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'm choosing a chocolate ice cream.
facts.explanation: choisis — je form of choisir (to choose). Like finir, choisir is a finir-type -ir verb: chois- + -is. Plural forms use the -iss- infix: choisissons, choisissent.

facts.quizQuestion:
Qu'est-ce que vous {___} ?
(What are you choosing?)
facts.explanation: choisissez — vous form of choisir. Finir-type plural forms insert -iss- before the ending: chois- + -iss- + -ez.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} maintenant.
(I'm leaving now.)
facts.explanation: pars — je form of partir (to leave). Partir-type -ir verbs drop the final consonant of the stem in singular: par- + -s. No -iss- infix.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} demain matin.
(They're leaving tomorrow morning.)
facts.explanation: partent — ils/elles form of partir. Partir-type plural: par- + -tent. No -iss- infix — the full stem part- restores the final consonant in plural.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} cet après-midi.
(I am going out this afternoon.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I am going out this afternoon.
facts.explanation: sors — je form of sortir (to go out). Like partir, sortir is a partir-type -ir verb: sor- + -s. The -iss- infix is absent in both singular and plural.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} ce soir.
(We're going out tonight.)
facts.explanation: sortons — nous form of sortir. Partir-type verbs restore the full stem in plural: sort- + -ons. No -iss- infix (contrast with finissons).

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} huit heures par nuit.
(I sleep eight hours a night.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I sleep eight hours a night.
facts.explanation: dors — je form of dormir (to sleep). Partir-type -ir verb: dor- + -s. The final -m of the stem dorm- is dropped in singular forms.

facts.quizQuestion:
Vous {___} bien ?
(Are you sleeping well?)
facts.explanation: dormez — vous form of dormir. Partir-type plural uses the full stem: dorm- + -ez. The full -m is restored in plural forms.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je ne {___} pas.
(I don't get it.)
facts.explanation: comprends — je form of comprendre (to understand). Prendre-type -re verbs: comprend- + -s in singular. The final -d is written but silent; -s is added in 1st and 2nd person singular.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je ne {___} pas l'allemand.
(I don't understand German.)
facts.explanation: comprends — je form of comprendre. Prendre-type: comprend- + -s. The conjugation is identical whether in affirmative or ne...pas negative constructions.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il ne {___} pas la question.
(He doesn't understand the question.)
facts.explanation: comprend — il/elle form of comprendre. No -s ending in 3rd person singular of -re verbs: the stem comprend- stands alone. The final -d is written but not pronounced.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} le bus tous les jours.
(I take the bus every day.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I take the bus every day.
facts.explanation: prends — je form of prendre (to take). Prendre-type -re verbs: prend- + -s. Stem changes to prenn- only in ils/elles form (prennent).

facts.quizQuestion:
Vous {___} un café ?
(Are you having a coffee?)
facts.explanation: prenez — vous form of prendre. Prendre-type plural uses a modified stem: pren- + -ez. This same stem pren- appears in prenons (nous form).

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} le métro pour aller au travail.
(They take the metro to go to work.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: They take the metro to go to work.
facts.explanation: prennent — ils/elles form of prendre. Unique stem change: prend- → prenn- + -ent. The double -nn- marks the ils/elles form and sets it apart from all other persons.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'{___} le bus depuis dix minutes.
(I've been waiting for the bus for ten minutes.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I've been waiting for the bus for ten minutes.
facts.explanation: attends — je form of attendre (to wait). Regular -re verb: attend- + -s. Unlike prendre, attendre keeps the full stem in all forms with no stem changes.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} nos amis devant le cinéma.
(We are waiting for our friends in front of the cinema.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We are waiting for our friends in front of the cinema.
facts.explanation: attendons — nous form of attendre. Regular -re verb plural: attend- + -ons. Full stem retained throughout with no irregularity.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu {___} la musique ?
(Can you hear the music?)
facts.explanation: entends — tu form of entendre (to hear). Regular -re verb: entend- + -s. Like attendre, entendre keeps the full stem in all persons with no stem changes.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il {___} bien avec ses nouvelles lunettes.
(He hears well with his new glasses.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He hears well with his new glasses.
facts.explanation: entend — il/elle form of entendre. Regular -re 3rd singular: bare stem with no added ending. The final -d is written but silent.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} ma voiture.
(I am selling my car.)
facts.explanation: vends — je form of vendre (to sell). Regular -re verb: vend- + -s. A third common -re verb alongside attendre and entendre that follows the same pattern throughout.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} des légumes au marché.
(They sell vegetables at the market.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: They sell vegetables at the market.
facts.explanation: vendent — ils/elles form of vendre. Regular -re plural: vend- + -ent. No stem change (unlike prennent), confirming the regular -re pattern.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} me baigner.
(I'm going to take a bath.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'm going to take a bath.
facts.explanation: vais — je form of aller as futur proche auxiliary. Structure: aller (present) + infinitive expresses a near or planned future action.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} le faire maintenant.
(I will do it right now.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I will do it right now.
facts.explanation: vais — je form of aller used as futur proche auxiliary before the infinitive faire. Expresses imminent action.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} essayer.
(I will try.)
facts.explanation: vais — je form of aller. Je vais + infinitive (essayer) = futur proche, expressing a planned near-future action.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu {___} prendre froid.
(You will catch cold.)
facts.explanation: vas — tu form of aller as futur proche auxiliary. Tu vas + infinitive (prendre) = near future, predicting what will happen.

facts.quizQuestion:
Demain, il {___} alunir.
(Tomorrow, he will land on the moon.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Tomorrow, he will land on the moon.
facts.explanation: va — il/elle form of aller as futur proche auxiliary. Il va + infinitive (alunir) = near future action scheduled for tomorrow.

facts.quizQuestion:
Emi {___} apprendre l'anglais.
(Emi will study English.)
facts.explanation: va — il/elle form of aller. Emi va + infinitive (apprendre) = futur proche expressing a future plan or intention.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} voyager cet été.
(We are going to travel this summer.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We are going to travel this summer.
facts.explanation: allons — nous form of aller as futur proche auxiliary. Nous allons + infinitive (voyager) = near future plan.

facts.quizQuestion:
Vous {___} arriver en retard.
(You are going to arrive late.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: You are going to arrive late.
facts.explanation: allez — vous form of aller as futur proche auxiliary. Vous allez + infinitive (arriver) = near future, expressing an imminent outcome.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ils {___} partir demain.
(They are going to leave tomorrow.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: They are going to leave tomorrow.
facts.explanation: vont — ils/elles form of aller as futur proche auxiliary. Ils vont + infinitive (partir) = near future plan or prediction.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} d'arriver.
(I just arrived.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: passé récent (je) — je viens de + infinitif
facts.explanation: viens — je form of venir as passé récent auxiliary. Structure: venir (present) + de + infinitive expresses an action just completed.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} d'avoir 20 ans.
(I have just turned 20.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: passé récent (je) — je viens de + infinitif
facts.explanation: viens — je form of venir. Je viens de + infinitive (avoir) = passé récent, describing something that has just happened.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il {___} juste d'arriver.
(He has just arrived home.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He has come home just now.
facts.grammarPointLabel: passé récent (il) — il vient de + infinitif
facts.explanation: vient — il/elle form of venir as passé récent auxiliary. Il vient de + infinitive (arriver) = just arrived, an action in the very recent past.

facts.quizQuestion:
Elle {___} de finir son travail.
(She has just finished her work.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She has just finished her work.
facts.grammarPointLabel: passé récent (elle) — elle vient de + infinitif
facts.explanation: vient — il/elle form of venir. Elle vient de + infinitive (finir) = passé récent expressing a recently completed action.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nous {___} de manger.
(We have just eaten.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: passé récent (nous) — nous venons de + infinitif
facts.explanation: venons — nous form of venir as passé récent auxiliary. Nous venons de + infinitive (manger) = we have just done something.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} un chat dans le jardin.
(There is a cat in the garden.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: There is a cat in the garden.
facts.explanation: il y a — fixed existential structure meaning 'there is / there are'. Invariable: il y a is used for both singular and plural nouns.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} deux livres sur la table.
(There are two books on the table.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: There are two books on the table.
facts.explanation: il y a — existential structure meaning 'there is/there are'. The form does not change for singular or plural: il y a un livre / il y a des livres.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il {___} aucun doute.
(There's no doubt.)
facts.explanation: n'y a — negative form of il y a. Full structure: il n'y a pas de + noun. Here aucun replaces pas de for stronger negation (not any at all).

facts.quizQuestion:
Il {___} aucune solution.
(There isn't any solution.)
facts.explanation: n'y a — the ne elides to n' before a vowel (y). Full form: il n'y a pas de / il n'y a aucune. Used to deny the existence of something.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} y aller.
(I'm going to go.)
facts.explanation: vais — je form of aller (to go). Irregular verb: vais, vas, va, allons, allez, vont. Also used as the futur proche auxiliary.

facts.quizQuestion:
Où {___}-tu ?
(Where are you going?)
facts.explanation: vas — tu form of aller. Irregular verb: the tu form adds -s. Used for literal movement or as futur proche auxiliary.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} au parc.
(Go to the park.)
facts.explanation: va — il/elle form of aller, also used as the informal singular imperative (Va ! = Go!). Irregular verb base form.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} habites-tu ?
(Where do you live?)
facts.explanation: où means 'where' and asks about place. It can begin a question directly: Où + subject + verb.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu sais {___} est la bibliothèque ?
(Do you know where the library is?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Do you know where the library is?
facts.grammarPointLabel: où — Mot interrogatif en milieu de phrase
facts.explanation: où also appears in indirect questions mid-sentence: Tu sais où… (Do you know where…).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} reviendras-tu ?
(When will you be back?)
facts.explanation: quand means 'when' and asks about time. With inversion: Quand + verb + subject pronoun?

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu sais {___} commence le cours ?
(Do you know when the class starts?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Do you know when the class starts?
facts.explanation: quand appears in indirect questions: Tu sais quand… (Do you know when…) — no inversion in the embedded clause.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} vas-tu ?
(How are you?)
facts.explanation: comment means 'how'. Comment vas-tu? is the classic French greeting asking how someone is doing.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tu t'appelles ?
(What is your name?)
facts.explanation: comment t'appelles-tu? is formal; comment tu t'appelles? is the informal version without inversion.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} demandes-tu ?
(Why do you ask?)
facts.explanation: pourquoi means 'why'. The typical answer begins with parce que (because).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} pleures-tu ?
(Why are you crying?)
facts.explanation: pourquoi with subject-verb inversion: Pourquoi + verb + subject? This is the formal interrogative form.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} mesures-tu ?
(How tall are you?)
facts.explanation: combien means 'how much' or 'how many'. Combien mesures-tu? asks about one's height in centimetres.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il y a {___} d'étudiants dans la classe ?
(How many students are in the class?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: How many students are in the class?
facts.grammarPointLabel: combien de — Mot interrogatif + de + nom
facts.explanation: combien de + noun asks 'how many/how much of something'. No article follows de: combien d'étudiants.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} heure est-il ?
(What time is it?)
facts.explanation: quel/quelle agrees with the noun it modifies. heure is feminine singular, so quelle: Quelle heure est-il?

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} âge as-tu ?
(How old are you?)
facts.explanation: quel is the masculine form of the interrogative adjective. âge is masculine, so quel: Quel âge as-tu?

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} est-elle ?
(Who is she?)
facts.explanation: qui means 'who' and refers to a person. It can be the subject (Qui est là?) or after a preposition (avec qui?).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} fais-tu ?
(What are you doing?)
facts.explanation: que means 'what' when the thing is the object of the verb. It requires inversion: Que + verb + subject?

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tu parles français ?
(Do you speak French?)
facts.grammarPointLabel: est-ce que — Tournure interrogative sans inversion
facts.explanation: est-ce que placed before subject + verb turns any statement into a question without requiring subject-verb inversion.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu parles de {___} ?
(What are you talking about?)
facts.explanation: quoi (what) is used after a preposition: de quoi, avec quoi, pour quoi. que cannot follow a preposition.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} parle pas japonais.
(I don't speak Japanese.)
facts.explanation: In ne…pas negation, ne comes immediately before the verb. ne stays ne before a consonant: ne parle.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je ne parle {___} japonais.
(I don't speak Japanese.)
facts.explanation: pas is the second element of ne…pas negation, placed directly after the conjugated verb.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___}ai pas de voiture.
(I don't have a car.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: n'…pas — Élision de ne devant voyelle
facts.explanation: ne elides to n' before a vowel or mute h: Je n'ai pas, Je n'aime pas — mandatory in written French.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je ne suis {___}.
(I don't follow.)
facts.explanation: ne…pas works with all verbs including être: Je ne suis pas means 'I am not' or 'I don't follow'.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je {___} comprends pas.
(I don't understand.)
facts.explanation: ne does not elide before a consonant: Je ne comprends pas — the full ne stays because comprends starts with c.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je ne comprends {___} l'allemand.
(I don't understand German.)
facts.explanation: pas precedes any direct object: Je ne comprends pas l'allemand — pas comes after the verb, before the object.

facts.quizQuestion:
Elle {___}est pas là.
(She is not here.)
facts.explanation: n' elides before est (starts with vowel): elle n'est pas — the apostrophe is mandatory in written French.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il ne mange {___} de viande.
(He doesn't eat meat.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: ne…pas de — Article indéfini supprimé après négation
facts.explanation: After ne…pas, un/une/des and partitive articles change to de/d': Il ne mange pas de viande (not 'de la viande').

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} sommes sept.
(There are seven of us.)
facts.explanation: nous — the first-person plural subject pronoun. Used as the grammatical subject; distinct from the tonic pronoun nous used after prepositions.

facts.quizQuestion:
Et {___} ?
(How about you?)
facts.grammarPointLabel: vous — Pronom sujet 2e pers. pl. / politesse
facts.explanation: vous — second-person plural (or formal singular) subject pronoun. Used to address multiple people or one person formally.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} mange pour vivre.
(We eat so we can live.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We eat so we can live.
facts.grammarPointLabel: on — Pronom sujet (= nous en style familier)
facts.explanation: on — third-person singular pronoun that in informal French equals nous. The verb always takes a third-person singular form: on mange, on part.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} parle français.
(I speak French.)
facts.explanation: je — first-person singular subject pronoun. It becomes j' before a vowel or mute h: j'aime, j'habite.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} s'appelle Tom.
(His name is Tom.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: il — Pronom sujet 3e pers. sg. masc.
facts.explanation: il — third-person masculine singular subject pronoun. Used for masculine nouns and male persons; contrasts with elle for feminine.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} est professeure.
(She is a teacher.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: elle — Pronom sujet 3e pers. sg. fém.
facts.explanation: elle — third-person feminine singular subject pronoun. Matches the gender of the person or feminine noun it refers to.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} habitent à Paris.
(They live in Paris.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: ils — Pronom sujet 3e pers. pl. masc.
facts.explanation: ils — third-person masculine plural subject pronoun. Also used for mixed-gender groups; elles is used for all-female groups.

facts.quizQuestion:
C'est {___}.
(It's me.)
facts.explanation: moi — tonic pronoun for je. Required after c'est (c'est moi), after prepositions (pour moi, avec moi), and for emphasis.

facts.quizQuestion:
Fais comme chez {___}.
(Make yourself at home.)
facts.explanation: toi — tonic pronoun required after the preposition chez. Subject pronouns (tu) cannot follow prepositions; only tonic forms can.

facts.quizQuestion:
C'est {___}, le professeur.
(He is the teacher.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: lui — Pronom tonique 3e pers. sg. masc.
facts.explanation: lui — tonic pronoun for il (masculine). Used after c'est (c'est lui), after prepositions (avec lui), and for emphasis or contrast.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je mange avec {___}.
(I eat with them.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: eux — Pronom tonique 3e pers. pl. masc.
facts.explanation: eux — tonic pronoun for ils (masculine or mixed group). Required after prepositions: avec eux, pour eux, chez eux.

facts.quizQuestion:
C'est {___} CD.
(It's my CD.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: mon — Possessif 1re pers. sg. + nom masculin
facts.explanation: mon — masculine singular possessive for my. Agrees with the noun possessed, not the speaker: CD is masculine, so mon (not ma) is used.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} grand-père aime marcher.
(My grandfather likes to walk.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: mon — Possessif 1re pers. sg. + nom masc. en tête de phrase
facts.explanation: mon — used before grand-père (masculine noun). Possessive adjectives in French agree with the gender and number of the object possessed, not the owner.

facts.quizQuestion:
C'est {___} tour.
(It's your move.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: ton — Possessif 2e pers. sg. + nom masculin
facts.explanation: ton — masculine singular possessive for your (informal). Tour is masculine, so ton is used; ta would be used with a feminine noun.

facts.quizQuestion:
Voici {___} mère.
(Here is my mother.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: ma — Possessif 1re pers. sg. + nom féminin
facts.explanation: ma — feminine singular possessive for my. Mère is feminine, so ma is used. Note: before feminine nouns beginning with a vowel, mon is used instead: mon amie.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il range {___} sac.
(He puts away his bag.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: son — Possessif 3e pers. sg. + nom masculin
facts.explanation: son — masculine singular possessive for his/her/its. French possessives agree with the noun (sac is masculine), not the gender of the owner — so son sac works for both a male and female owner.

facts.quizQuestion:
Elle aime {___} chats.
(She loves her cats.)
facts.explanation: ses — plural possessive for his/her/its. Used regardless of the owner's gender when the possessed noun is plural: ses chats (his or her cats).

facts.quizQuestion:
C'est {___} école.
(That is our school.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: notre — Possessif 1re pers. pl. + nom sg.
facts.explanation: notre — singular possessive for our. Used before any singular noun regardless of gender; nos is used before plural nouns: nos écoles.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'aime {___} voiture.
(I like your car.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: votre — Possessif 2e pers. pl. + nom sg.
facts.explanation: votre — singular possessive for your (plural/formal). Voiture is singular, so votre is used; vos would be used with plural nouns: vos voitures.

facts.quizQuestion:
C'est {___} maison.
(It is their house.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: leur — Possessif 3e pers. pl. + nom sg.
facts.explanation: leur — singular possessive for their. Invariable before singular nouns (masculine or feminine): leur maison, leur jardin. Becomes leurs before plural nouns.

facts.quizQuestion:
Voici {___} amis.
(Here are our friends.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: possessive nos — plural noun, 1st person plural
facts.explanation: nos — plural form of notre. Nos amis = our friends.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'aime {___} idées.
(I like your [plural] ideas.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: possessive vos — plural noun, 2nd person plural
facts.explanation: vos — plural form of votre. Vos idées = your ideas.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'étudie {___} anglais chez moi.
(I study English at home.)
facts.explanation: l' — elided form of le/la before a vowel or mute h. Anglais starts with a vowel, so le anglais → l'anglais.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je n'aime pas {___} école.
(I don't like school.)
facts.explanation: l' — elided definite article before école (feminine noun starting with a vowel). La école → l'école.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je lis {___} journal chaque matin.
(I read the newspaper every morning.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I read the newspaper every morning.
facts.explanation: le — definite article for masculine singular nouns. Journal (newspaper) is masculine, so le journal.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu aimes {___} musique classique ?
(Do you like classical music?)
facts.explanation: la — definite article for feminine singular nouns. Musique (music) is feminine, so la musique.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'aime {___} chiens.
(I like dogs.)
facts.explanation: les — definite article for all plural nouns, regardless of gender. Chiens (dogs) is masculine plural, so les chiens.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'ai {___} voiture.
(I have a car.)
facts.explanation: une — indefinite article for feminine singular nouns. Voiture (car) is feminine, so une voiture.

facts.quizQuestion:
C'est {___} surprise !
(It's a surprise!)
facts.explanation: une — indefinite article for feminine singular nouns. Surprise is feminine, so une surprise.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'ai {___} idée !
(I have an idea!)
facts.explanation: une — indefinite article for feminine singular nouns. Idée (idea) is feminine and starts with a vowel, but une does NOT elide — only definite la → l' before vowels.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je mange {___} pomme.
(I am eating an apple.)
facts.explanation: une — indefinite article for feminine singular nouns. Pomme (apple) is feminine, so une pomme.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'ai {___} chien.
(I have a dog.)
facts.explanation: un — indefinite article for masculine singular nouns. Chien (dog) is masculine, so un chien.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu bois {___} thé ?
(Do you drink tea?)
facts.explanation: du — partitive article meaning 'some' for uncountable masculine nouns. Thé (tea) is masculine, so du thé. Du is a contraction of de + le.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je veux {___} eau.
(I want some water.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: Partitive article — de l' before vowel
facts.explanation: de l' — partitive article before a vowel or mute h. Eau (water) is feminine and starts with a vowel, so de la → de l'eau.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'écoute {___} musique.
(I listen to music.)
facts.explanation: de la — partitive article for uncountable feminine nouns. Musique (music) is feminine and starts with a consonant, so de la musique.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je mange {___} pain le matin.
(I eat bread in the morning.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I eat bread in the morning.
facts.explanation: du — partitive article for uncountable masculine nouns. Pain (bread) is masculine, so du pain. Du is a contraction of de + le.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'adore {___} publicité !
(I love that commercial!)
facts.explanation: cette — demonstrative adjective for feminine singular nouns. Publicité (advertisement) is feminine, so cette publicité. Use cette before any feminine noun regardless of whether it starts with a vowel.

facts.quizQuestion:
Regarde {___} maison !
(Look at that house!)
facts.explanation: cette — demonstrative adjective for feminine singular nouns. Maison (house) is feminine, so cette maison.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'aime beaucoup {___} histoires.
(I really like these stories.)
facts.explanation: ces — demonstrative adjective for all plural nouns. Histoires (stories) is feminine plural, so ces histoires. Ces covers both masculine and feminine plurals.

facts.quizQuestion:
Tu connais {___} garçon ?
(Do you know that boy?)
facts.explanation: ce — demonstrative adjective for masculine singular nouns starting with a consonant. Garçon (boy) is masculine and starts with a consonant, so ce garçon. Before a vowel or mute h, use cet instead.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je n'aime pas {___} homme.
(I don't like that man.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: Demonstrative adjective — cet (masculine before vowel/mute h)
facts.explanation: cet — special form of ce used before masculine nouns starting with a vowel or mute h. Homme (man) is masculine and starts with a mute h, so cet homme. This avoids collision of two vowel sounds.

facts.quizQuestion:
Montez {___} escaliers !
(Go up these stairs!)
facts.explanation: ces — demonstrative adjective for all plural nouns. Escaliers (stairs) is masculine plural, so ces escaliers.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je mange {___} légumes.
(I am eating [some] vegetables.)
facts.explanation: des — partitive plural, used for unspecified quantity of countable/uncountable items.

facts.quizQuestion:
Il boit {___} café le matin.
(He drinks [some] coffee in the morning.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He drinks coffee in the morning.
facts.explanation: du — partitive masculine, used for unspecified quantity of a mass noun.

facts.quizQuestion:
J'habite {___} Paris.
(I live in Paris.)
facts.explanation: à — used before city names for location or residence: à Paris, à Lyon, à Rome. Rule: à + ville.

facts.quizQuestion:
Viens {___} la maison !
(Come to the house!)
facts.explanation: à — marks destination or location: à la maison (at home). Contracts with le: à + le = au; with les: à + les = aux.

facts.quizQuestion:
Je vis {___} ville.
(I live in the city.)
facts.explanation: en — used in fixed location expressions (en ville, en classe) and before feminine country names. No article follows: en ville, not en la ville.

facts.quizQuestion:
Elle habite {___} France.
(She lives in France.)
facts.explanation: en — used before feminine country names (most ending in -e): en France, en Espagne, en Italie. Compare: au + masculine country (au Japon, au Canada).

facts.quizQuestion:
Le chat est {___} la boîte.
(The cat is inside the box.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The cat is inside the box.
facts.explanation: dans — means inside a defined, enclosed space: dans la boîte, dans la salle, dans le tiroir. Always followed by a noun with an article.

facts.quizQuestion:
Posez-le {___} la table.
(Lay it on the table.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: sur — Preposition (on top of a surface)
facts.explanation: sur — means on or on top of a surface: sur la table, sur le lit, sur le bureau. Contrast with sous (under).

facts.quizQuestion:
Le chien dort {___} la chaise.
(The dog is sleeping under the chair.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The dog is sleeping under the chair.
facts.explanation: sous — means under or beneath: sous la chaise, sous le lit, sous la table. Contrast with sur (on top of).

facts.quizQuestion:
Je t'attends {___} l'école.
(I'm waiting for you in front of the school.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'm waiting for you in front of the school.
facts.explanation: devant — means in front of: devant l'école, devant la porte, devant la maison. Contrast with derrière (behind).

facts.quizQuestion:
Le jardin est {___} la maison.
(The garden is behind the house.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The garden is behind the house.
facts.explanation: derrière — means behind: derrière la maison, derrière l'école. Contrast with devant (in front of).

facts.quizQuestion:
J'étudie l'anglais {___} moi.
(I study English at home.)
facts.explanation: chez — means at the home or place of someone: chez moi (at my place), chez Marie, chez le médecin (at the doctor's). Always followed by a person or tonic pronoun, never a place noun.

facts.quizQuestion:
On se retrouve {___} Marc ce soir.
(We're meeting at Marc's place tonight.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We're meeting at Marc's place tonight.
facts.grammarPointLabel: chez — Preposition (at a named person's place)
facts.explanation: chez — used with proper names and pronouns, never with place nouns: chez Marc, chez elle. Contrast: à la bibliothèque (place noun requires à + article).

facts.quizQuestion:
Je viens {___} mes amis.
(I'm coming with my friends.)
facts.grammarPointLabel: avec — Preposition (with / in the company of)
facts.explanation: avec — means with (accompaniment or manner): avec mes amis, avec plaisir. Opposite: sans (without).

facts.quizQuestion:
Ce cadeau est {___} toi.
(This gift is for you.)
facts.explanation: pour — marks purpose, destination, or beneficiary: pour toi, pour le travail, partir pour Paris. Use pour when something is intended for someone or aimed at a goal.

facts.quizQuestion:
— Qui est-ce ? — {___} mon professeur.
(— Who is it? — It's my teacher.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: — Who is it? — It's my teacher.
facts.explanation: c'est — for identifying a person when followed by any determiner (possessive, article): c'est mon professeur, c'est une amie. When introducing who someone is, always c'est (not il/elle est) before a noun with a determiner.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} enseignant.
(He is a teacher. [describing profession without article])
facts.grammarPointLabel: il est — Description (il est + bare profession/adjective)
facts.explanation: il est / elle est — used before a bare adjective or unmodified profession noun (no article): il est enseignant, elle est médecin, il est grand. Contrast: c'est un enseignant (with article = identification).

chainThemes.name: Present: -er Verbs
chainThemes.description: Regular present indicative conjugations for -ER verbs: parler, aimer, travailler, habiter

chainThemes.name: Être & Avoir
chainThemes.description: The two essential irregular verbs — être and avoir — backbone of all compound tenses

chainThemes.name: Articles & Genre
chainThemes.description: Definite, indefinite, and partitive articles plus noun gender and agreement

chainThemes.name: Pronoms & Possessifs
chainThemes.description: Subject pronouns, tonic pronouns, possessive adjectives, and demonstratives

chainThemes.name: Questions & Négation
chainThemes.description: Interrogative words (où, quand, comment…) and basic negation with ne…pas

chainThemes.name: Present: -ir et -re
chainThemes.description: Regular and common -IR verbs (finir, partir) and -RE verbs (prendre, comprendre)

chainThemes.name: Temps Périphrastiques
chainThemes.description: Near future (aller + inf), recent past (venir de + inf), and il y a existence

chainThemes.name: Prépositions & Structure
chainThemes.description: Prepositions of place and time (à, en, dans, chez, sur…) and c'est/il est patterns

answerTypePools.description: Present indicative -er verb conjugations (parler, aimer, habiter, travailler, écouter, etc.)
Present indicative conjugations of être
Present indicative conjugations of avoir
Present indicative conjugations of aller
Present indicative -ir verb conjugations (finir-type with -iss- and partir-type without)
Present indicative -re verb conjugations (attendre, prendre, comprendre)
Pronominal/reflexive verb forms: s'appeler, se lever, se coucher, se réveiller
Definite articles le, la, l', les and obligatory contractions au, aux, du, des
Indefinite articles un, une, des
Partitive articles du, de la, de l', and de/d' after negation
Singular possessive adjectives: mon/ma/mes, ton/ta/tes, son/sa/ses
Plural possessive adjectives: notre/nos, votre/vos, leur/leurs
Demonstrative adjectives: ce, cet (before vowel), cette, ces
Subject pronouns: je, tu, il, elle, on, nous, vous, ils, elles
Tonic/disjunctive pronouns: moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux, elles
Question words: où, quand, comment, pourquoi, combien, quel/quelle, qui, que/qu'
Basic negation structures with ne…pas (and n'…pas before vowel)
Futur proche: aller (present) + infinitif — expressing planned or imminent actions
Passé récent: venir de (present) + infinitif — expressing a just-completed action
Prepositions of place and movement: à, de, en, dans, sur, sous, chez, devant, derrière, avec, pour
